perturbation.original_text: The Parties acknowledge that Anaheim is a public entity subject to the California Public Records Act, and the Ralph M. Brown Act; except to the extent permitted by law, the Parties may, without violating this Agreement, disclose matters that are made confidential by this Agreement to governmental officials or the public as required by any law, regulation, order, rule, ruling or other requirement of law (“Law”).
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties acknowledge that Anaheim is a public entity subject to the California Public Records Act, and the Ralph M. Brown Act; the Parties may, at their discretion, disclose matters that are made confidential by this Agreement to governmental officials or the public as required by any law, regulation, order, rule, ruling or other requirement of law (“Law”).
perturbation.explanation: By changing "except to the extent permitted by law" to "at their discretion", the modified text contradicts the mandatory disclosure requirements of the California Public Records Act and the Ralph M. Brown Act for public entities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Public Records Act (Cal. Gov. Code § 6250 et seq.)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Public Records Act mandates public access to government records, with specific exceptions. The modification grants Anaheim discretionary power to disclose confidential information, which conflicts with the CPRA's requirement for disclosure unless a specific exemption applies. Section 6253 mandates that public records are open to inspection at all times during office hours and every person has a right to inspect any record. This modification removes the mandatory element and replaces it with a discretionary power, contradicting the CPRA.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Cal Cities Oh no! Sorry about that. Can’t seem to find that page. Let’s see if we can help you get to where you want to be. Home Advocacy Education and Events Get Involved News Resources About Us
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the mandatory disclosure requirements under the California Public Records Act (CPRA) to a discretionary action. The original text states disclosure can happen "except to the extent permitted by law", acknowledging legal limits on confidentiality. The changed text, however, grants the Parties the power to disclose "at their discretion", conflicting with the CPRA's mandate for public access unless a specific exemption applies. Scraped Law Snippet 1 points to GOV 6253 in the California Code, implying the existence of mandatory access provisions which are undermined by the 'at their discretion' modification, fitting the definition of a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient will disclose only such information as it determines is legally required and will use commercially reasonable efforts to obtain confidential treatment for any Confidential Information that is so disclosed. Recipient is not required to seek or obtain a judicial determination establishing confidential treatment, but it will cooperated in any efforts to do so by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will disclose only such information as it deems necessary and will make no effort to obtain confidential treatment for any Confidential Information that is so disclosed. Recipient is not required to seek or obtain a judicial determination establishing confidential treatment, nor will they cooperate in any efforts to do so by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "legally required" to "deems necessary" and removing the requirement to use commercially reasonable efforts to obtain confidential treatment, the modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting legal standards for protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426 et seq.)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets from misappropriation. While this agreement isn't directly about trade secrets, confidential information can fall under that definition. By removing the obligation to use 'commercially reasonable efforts' to protect confidential information and replacing 'legally required' with 'deems necessary' this weakens protections below what is expected under CUTSA, particularly if the confidential information could be considered a trade secret. 3426.1 defines what is misappropriation, and by lessening the standard of care, the recipient of the confidential information can misappropriate it easily.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the protection of confidential information, creating a legal contradiction. The original text requires the recipient to disclose only information 'legally required' and to use 'commercially reasonable efforts' to obtain confidential treatment. The changed text replaces 'legally required' with 'deems necessary' and eliminates the requirement to use 'commercially reasonable efforts'. According to the Law Explanation, this lessening of the standard of care could lead to misappropriation, especially if the confidential information is considered a trade secret. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' and 'improper means', which includes breach of a duty to maintain secrecy. By removing the 'commercially reasonable efforts' requirement, the modified text potentially allows for easier misappropriation because the recipient is not held to a reasonable standard of care in protecting the information, thus contradicting the legal obligation to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is made under, and shall be construed according to, the substantive laws of the State of California , without regard to the conflict of laws provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is made under, and shall be construed according to, the substantive laws of the State of Nevada, without regard to the conflict of laws provisions.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the governing law from California to Nevada, this could potentially contradict the reasonable expectations of the parties, especially if the contract is centered around activities or property located in California.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1646 indicates that a contract is interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed or, if that is not indicated, where it was made. Given the context of the original contract (Anaheim, California, SONGS), applying Nevada law could contradict California's choice-of-law principles, especially if the contract's performance is primarily in California. Changing the jurisdiction creates uncertainty in enforcement and interpretation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ll contracts, whether public or private, are to be interpreted by the same rules, except as otherwise provided by this Code. 1636. A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. 1637. For the purpose of ascertaining the intention of the parties to a contract, if otherwise doubtful, the rules given in this Chapter are to be applied. 1638. The language of a contract is to govern its interpretation, if the language is clear and explicit, and does not involve an absurdity. 1639. When a contract is reduced to writing, the intention of the parties is to be ascertained from the writing alone, if possible; subject, however, to the other provisions of this Title. 1640. When, through fraud, mistake, or accident, a written contract fails to express the real intention of the parties, such intention is to be regarded, and the erroneous parts of the writing disregarded. 1641. The whole of a contract is to be taken together, so as to give effect to every part, if reasonably practicable, each clause helping to interpret the other. 1642. Several contracts relating to the same matters, between the same parties, and made as parts of substantially one transaction, are to be taken together. 1643. A contract must receive such an interpretation as will make it lawful, operative, definite, reasonable, and capable of being carried into effect, if it can be done without violating the intention of the parties. 1644. The words of a contract are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense, rather than according to their strict legal meaning; unless used by the parties in a technical sense, or unless a special meaning is given to them by usage, in which case the latter must be followed. 1645. Technical words are to be interpreted as usually understood by persons in the profession or business to which they relate, unless clearly used in a different sense. 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. 1646.5. Notwithstanding Section 1646, the parties to any contract, agreement, or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, relating to a transaction involving in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars ($250,000), including a transaction otherwise covered by subdivision (1) of Section 1105 of the Commercial Code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not the contract, agreement, or undertaking or transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section does not apply to any contract, agreement, or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subdivision (2) of Section 1105 of the Commercial Code. This section applies to contracts, agreements, and undertakings entered into before, on, or after its effective date; it shall be fully retroactive. Contracts, agreements, and undertakings selecting California law entered into before the effective date of this section shall be valid, enforceable, and effective as if this section had been in effect on the date they were entered into; and actions and proceedings commencing in a court of this state before the effective date of this section may be maintained as if this section were in effect on the date they were commenced. 1647. A contract may be explained by reference to the circumstances under which it was made, and the matter to which it relates. 1648. However broad may be the terms of a contract, it extends only to those things concerning which it appears that the parties intended to contract. 1649. If the terms of a promise are in any respect ambiguous or uncertain, it mus
perturbation.justification: The change from California to Nevada law could contradict the principle outlined in California Civil Code Section 1646, as mentioned in the Law Explanation. Section 1646 states that a contract should be interpreted according to the law of the place where it is to be performed or made. If the contract's performance or creation is primarily in California, applying Nevada law would contradict California's choice-of-law principles. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight the potential contradiction with California's choice-of-law principles if the contract's performance is centered in California.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient will return to the Disclosing Party , upon demand or in the event either Party ceases to be interested in pursuing the Purpose, all Confidential Information provided to Recipient, including all copies thereof which may have been made by or on behalf of Recipient, and Recipient shall destroy, or cause to be destroyed, all notes or memoranda or other stored information of any kind prepared by Recipient relating to the Confi dential Information or discussions generally.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will retain all Confidential Information provided to Recipient indefinitely, including all copies thereof which may have been made by or on behalf of Recipient, and Recipient is not required to destroy any notes or memoranda or other stored information of any kind prepared by Recipient relating to the Confidential Information or discussions generally.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "return" to "retain" and removing the obligation to destroy confidential information, the modified text potentially contradicts trade secret law and the general duty to protect confidential information after the purpose of the agreement has ended.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426 et seq.) and common law duty of confidentiality
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets from misappropriation. While the agreement isn't explicitly about trade secrets, the unlimited retention and lack of destruction of confidential information could be considered a violation of CUTSA if the information qualifies as a trade secret. The modification allows the recipient to keep and potentially use the information indefinitely, which could be seen as an ongoing misappropriation. Also, after the end of the agreement, there is a common law duty to protect Confidential Information which is being contradicted by this sentence. 3426.1 defines misappropriation, and retaining the confidential information would amount to such.

As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) as it relates to misappropriation. The original text requires the recipient to return or destroy confidential information upon demand or termination of the agreement. The changed text allows the recipient to retain the information indefinitely, which could be considered 'use' under CUTSA if the information qualifies as a trade secret. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CIV 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' as including 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. The modified text implies consent to retain and potentially use the confidential information indefinitely, which contradicts the duty to protect confidential information, especially if it's considered a trade secret.
